coredata.dc:title: A novel ultrasonic method to exfoliate CLDH toward improving mechanical and chloride binding properties of cementitious composites
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Wu-Jian
Liu, Jian-Wei
Zheng, Shu-Yi
He, Chuang
coredata.dc:description: With the complexity of architectural structures increasing, traditional cement-based materials inevitably face challenges of mechanical performance and durability. Calcined layered double hydroxide (CLDH) attracts significant attention due to its potential for improving mechanical and chloride binding properties of cement-based composites, but suffers from poor dispersion, limiting its full potential. To tackle this issue, a novel method utilizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in conjunction with ultrasound has been developed to efficiently exfoliate CLDH, thereby enhancing the mechanical and chloride binding properties of cement composites. Most importantly, its related enhancement mechanisms were revealed by comprehensively analyzing hydration heat evolutions, phase compositions, and microstructures of relevant cement samples. Specifically, highly dispersed CLDH (G-CLDH) prepared by GQDs-assisted ultrasonication possessed a larger specific surface area (S BET), significantly increasing by 92.04 % compared with CLDH treated by pure ultrasonication (i.e., U-CLDH). After incorporating 0.8 wt.% (by weight of cement) G-CLDH, the cement composites demonstrated 22.29 % and 58.56 % improvement in compressive strength and chloride binding capacity respectively than blank group. The enhancement mechanism of compressive strength and chloride-binding capacity by G-CLDH was rationally proposed. As for the former, it was attributed to a significant reduction of porosity and a remarkable increase of hydrated calcium silicate (C-S-H) gels; Regarding the latter, that was a larger number of C-S-H gels and better dispersed G-CLDH led to more physically adsorbed chloride and more ion-exchanged-caused bound chloride respectively. The developed innovative approach could prepare high-dispersion CLDH to simultaneously enhance mechanical performance and chloride binding capacity of cementitious materials, promoting to the advancement of high-performance cement composites.

head.ce:title: A novel ultrasonic method to exfoliate CLDH toward improving mechanical and chloride binding properties of cementitious composites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the complexity of architectural structures increasing, traditional cement-based materials inevitably face challenges of mechanical performance and durability. Calcined layered double hydroxide (CLDH) attracts significant attention due to its potential for improving mechanical and chloride binding properties of cement-based composites, but suffers from poor dispersion, limiting its full potential. To tackle this issue, a novel method utilizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in conjunction with ultrasound has been developed to efficiently exfoliate CLDH, thereby enhancing the mechanical and chloride binding properties of cement composites. Most importantly, its related enhancement mechanisms were revealed by comprehensively analyzing hydration heat evolutions, phase compositions, and microstructures of relevant cement samples. Specifically, highly dispersed CLDH (G-CLDH) prepared by GQDs-assisted ultrasonication possessed a larger specific surface area (S BET), significantly increasing by 92.04 % compared with CLDH treated by pure ultrasonication (i.e., U-CLDH). After incorporating 0.8 wt.% (by weight of cement) G-CLDH, the cement composites demonstrated 22.29 % and 58.56 % improvement in compressive strength and chloride binding capacity respectively than blank group. The enhancement mechanism of compressive strength and chloride-binding capacity by G-CLDH was rationally proposed. As for the former, it was attributed to a significant reduction of porosity and a remarkable increase of hydrated calcium silicate (C-S-H) gels; Regarding the latter, that was a larger number of C-S-H gels and better dispersed G-CLDH led to more physically adsorbed chloride and more ion-exchanged-caused bound chloride respectively. The developed innovative approach could prepare high-dispersion CLDH to simultaneously enhance mechanical performance and chloride binding capacity of cementitious materials, promoting to the advancement of high-performance cement composites.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cement, an indispensable material in the construction industry, finds extensive usage in diverse buildings and infrastructures (Chi et al., 2023; Jiang et al., 2022; Jiang et al., 2023a; Lu, D. et al., 2023a). With the rapid development of society, architectural structures become gradually intricate, leading to a rising demand for mechanical properties and durability (Jakubovskis et al., 2020; Jiang et al., 2023b; Zhang et al., 2022). However, traditional cement materials are difficult to meet these requirements, potentially affecting the safety and longevity of structures (Ma et al., 2005). To improve the above performance of traditional cement materials, researchers have proposed various enhancement methods. Among them, the addition of nanomaterials including nano SiO2 (Liu et al., 2023), TiO2 (Joshaghani et al., 2020), Al2O3 (Feng et al., 2021), graphene oxide (Lu, C. et al., 2023; Lu, D. et al., 2023c; Lu, D. et al., 2023d), carbon nanotubes (Cui et al., 2017; Lu, D. et al., 2023b; Praseeda and Rao, 2022), GQDs (Long et al., 2023b) and layered double hydroxide (LDH) (Dong et al., 2023; Qu et al., 2018) is one of the most effective methods, benefitting from their high specific surface area and active sites (Zuo et al., 2023). On the one hand, nanomaterials can substantially improve the mechanical properties of cement composites at low dosages. On the other hand, as is well known, chloride corrosion is the primary factor reducing the durability performance of reinforced concrete (RC) structures (Hu and Poon, 2022). Improving the corrosion resistance of chloride in cement composites is considered as both feasible and effective by the chloride binding technology (Ming et al., 2023). Nanomaterials are also employed to enhance the chloride binding capacity of cement. Among these nanomaterials, LDH has attracted much attention by virtue of its interlayer anion exchange properties and nucleation effect (Pang et al., 2022).

LDH is a two-dimensional functional nanomaterial exhibiting anion exchange capability and nano-nucleation effect, and shows great potential to enhance both mechanical and chloride binding capacity, improving the safety and lifespan of RC structures (Dong et al., 2023; Hong et al., 2023; Yang, H. et al., 2021). Besides the stronger thermal stability and more active sites than LDH, Calcined LDH (CLDH) also possesses structural memory effect (Xiang et al., 2022; Yoon et al., 2014). Hence, CLDH has also been applied to cement composites to further improve mechanical and chloride binding capacity (Yoon et al., 2014). However, LDH/CLDH is subject to poor dispersion, enormously limiting its extensive employment (Yang, L. et al., 2021b; Zhou et al., 2022). Consequently, to maximize the excellent properties of LDH/CLDH, multitudes of techniques have been developed to boost its dispersion, such as surface modification (Du et al., 2018; Xie et al., 2016), ultrasonic treatment (Rahman et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2023), and in situ growth (Huang et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2022a; Zhou et al., 2022). In detail, Du et al. modified LDH using camphorsulfonic acid-doped polyaniline, and found that the dispersibility of LDH in aqueous solution was significantly improved. Subsequently, the modified LDH was applied to waterborne epoxy resin, illustrating excellent corrosion resistance (Du et al., 2018). Rahman et al. directly dispersed LDH in aqueous solution by ultrasonic treatment, and revealed that the dispersed LDH presented higher adsorption capacity than the original LDH, effectively removing heavy metal ions from wastewater (Rahman et al., 2018). Xu’s group successfully grew LDH on zeolite powder and Nano-SiO2 substrate, creating novel zeolite@LDHs and Nano-SiO2@LDHs composites. This effectively avoided LDHs agglomeration, and enhanced the chloride binding capacity of cementitious composites (Wang et al., 2022a; Zhou et al., 2022). Nonetheless, few above-mentioned strategies are considered as an ideal dispersion method on account of their inevitable shortcomings involving harsh reaction conditions, difficult operation and time consumption, energy consumption, and susceptibility to environmental factors. More importantly, there is little research focusing on the dispersion approaches for CLDH, resulting in its limited application in cement matrix. Therefore, it is urgently required to develop a simple and efficient method to disperse CLDH, and explore its effect on the cementitious composites.

Exhilaratingly, a novel method utilizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in conjunction with ultrasound has been successfully proposed to efficiently disperse 2D nanomaterials (e.g., GO, MoS2, h-BN, WS2, and C3N4) (Gong et al., 2021; Long et al., 2022a; Wan et al., 2022; Yan et al., 2020; Yin et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2019). This method provides a novel, simple, efficient and eco-friendly way for dispersion of 2D nanomaterials, and even achieves a stripping efficiency of 100 % (Zhou et al., 2019). It is worth mentioning that GQDs are a new category of zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, typically with a size of less than 10 nm and an amphipathic structure consisting of several functional groups on the surface of a single or multiple-layer graphene core. GQDs have been widely employed in biological medicine, corrosion protection, catalytic reactions, optoelectronic devices and lubrication owing to their advantages of simple synthesis, low cost, high dispersibility, excellent water solubility and environmental friendliness (E et al., 2021; E et al., 2023; He et al., 2021; He et al., 2022a; He et al., 2022b; He et al., 2023a; He et al., 2023b; Li et al., 2023; Lv et al., 2023). Nevertheless, up to now, no studies have been conducted to investigate the impact of ultrasonic dispersion with GQDs assistance for CLDH, not to speak of the effects on the properties within cement composites of the as-treated CLDH.

To address this issue, this study initially employed a novel GQDs-assisted ultrasonic method for dispersing CLDH. The as-obtained CLDH (G-CLDH) was then comprehensively examined to determine its impact on the mechanical and chloride binding properties of cement materials. Crucially, the related enhancement mechanisms were uncovered. Specifically, the dispersion behaviors of different CLDHs including G-CLDH, U-CLDH (ultrasonic treatment CLDH without GQDs) and untreated CLDH were disclosed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), N2 adsorption-desorption measurements and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) theory. Subsequently, the mechanical and chloride binding properties of cementitious composites modified by 0.8 wt.% CLDH/U-CLDH/G-CLDH were confirmed by compressive strength tests and equilibrium experiments respectively. This verified that G-CLDH presented an optimum effect, significantly increasing the compressive strength by 22.29 % for the 28-d curing mortar and chloride binding capacity of the cement pastes increased by 58.56 % after immersion for 28 d in 3 M NaCl solution compared with the blank group, respectively. More importantly, the enhancement mechanisms induced by G-CLDH were revealed by comprehensively analyzing hydration heat evolutions, phase compositions, and microstructures of various cement composites. The proposed dispersion method of GQDs-assisted ultrasonication would contribute to better employment of CLDH and CLDH-like nanomaterials in cement composites, thus promoting the development of high-performance cementitious composites.

Commercial hydrotalcite (Mg6Al2(OH)16CO3·4H2O, namely Mg/Al-CO3 LDH) with a purity of 98 % was obtained from Heowns Opde Technologies, Ltd. (Tianjin, China), and used without further purification. Calcined LDH (CLDH) was prepared by following the method described in the reported literature (Zhou et al., 2011). In detail, LDH was calcined at 500 °C for 3 h in a muffle furnace; And cooled to room temperature, the resulting powder was CLDH. By calcining hydrotalcite (Mg/Al-CO3 LDH) under high temperature, its structure was broken, forming an oxide according to Equation (1).

Portland cement (PC) with PI 52.5R conforming to the Chinese Standard GB175 and GB176 was employed, and its physical properties and chemical composition were provided in Tables 1 and 2
, respectively. Additionally, naphthalene, concentrated nitric acid (HNO3) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., and no further purification required. Deionized (DI) water with a conductivity of 18 MΩ cm−1 was used throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs, U-CLDH and G-CLDH
ce:section.ce:para: A one-pot hydrothermal method described in the previous literature (Wang et al., 2014) was moderately adapted to synthesize GQDs. Specifically, 3.0 g naphthalene was added into a beaker containing 100 mL of concentrated HNO3 and kept under magnetic stirring for 10 h under 80 °C. Subsequently, the resultant mixture underwent centrifugation, washing, and drying to yield approximately 4.0 g nitronaphthalene powder. Further, 1.5 g nitronaphthalene powder was incorporated into 120 mL 0.2 M NaOH solution, and then heated at 200 °C for 8 h in a 150 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture proceeded a series of procedures including neutralization, high-speed centrifugation, filtration (0.2 μm filter), and dialysis to eliminate unreacted small molecules, sodium salts, and insoluble carbon-based products. Finally, the brownish powdered GQDs were obtained through rotary evaporation and freeze-drying.

Highly dispersed CLDH referred to as G-CLDH, was produced through GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation with the following procedure: 8 g CLDH was added to a 500 mL aqueous solution containing GQDs at a concentration of 0.32 mg/mL (CLDH: GQDs was 25:1). Among them, the selection of a 25:1 mass ratio was based on referencing previous studies and appropriate adjustment (Long et al., 2022a, 2023a). Subsequently, the solution was ultrasonicated for 30 min at 10 °C in a water bath with ultrasound parameters of 720 W and 20 kHz. A portion of the mixture was then frozen and dried to obtain a light brown G-CLDH powder for characterization. And the remaining mixed solution was employed for the preparation of cementitious specimens. U-CLDH and G-CLDH had the same preparation process, only without the utilization of GQDs. A schematic representation of the preparation for G-CLDH and U-CLDH was presented in Fig. 1 .

Yang et al. (Yang, L. et al., 2021a) demonstrated that incorporating 0.8 wt.% (by weight of high sulphate-resistant PC) LDH significantly enhanced the compressive strength of mortar specimens. Given that, the incorporation of each CLDH into cement composites was chosen as 0.8 wt.%. These specimens containing GQDs (0.032 wt.%), CLDH, U-CLDH and G-CLDH were prepared under identical conditions, and denoted as G, C, UC, and GC groups, respectively. Their preparation process was as described below: At first, 250 mL aqueous solution consisting of 4 g CLDH, U-CLDH or G-CLDH were obtained through ultrasonic treatment. Following an initial 30 s of low-speed mixing of sand and cement in a container, the aqueous solution was added to the mixture (The ratio of water to cement (w/c) was fixed at 0.5), and the mixtures was followed by an additional 30 s of low-speed, succeeded by 30 s high-speed mixing, with a 90 s pause, and finally 60 s high-speed mixing. Notably, no other admixtures were introduced. Then, the resulting mortars were poured into the molds of size 40 mm × 40 mm × 40 mm. Subsequently, the mortar specimens were located in a curing chamber at 20 ± 2 °C and a relative humidity of more than 95 %. After 24 h, the specimens were demolded and kept in the same environment up to the specified compressive testing age. Similar preparation procedures were used for blank group (labeled as B group), but only consisted of DI water. The mix proportions of mortars were presented in Table 3 . Additionally, to comprehensively explore the effect of different CLDHs on the chloride binding capacity, phase composition, and microstructure of the cementitious composites, cement pastes specimens were prepared following a same procedure as mortar samples, excluding the addition of sand. Cement paste specimens with dimensions of 15 mm × 15 mm × 15 mm were cured under the same conditions as mortars.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of GQDs, LDH and CLDHs
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the information of their diameter and morphology, the prepared GQDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) images taken on FEI Talos F200S (USA) and Brucker AFM (Germany), respectively. X-ray diffractometry (XRD, Brucker D8 Advance, Germany), Raman spectra (Thermo Scientific DXR, USA) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet-6700, USA) were employed to obtain information about the structural composition of GQDs. Furthermore, XRD and FTIR were used to analyze the structural composition of LDH and three types of CLDHs with different treatments. Additionally, the structural composition and morphology of were investigated various CLDHs using TEM, XRD, and FTIR. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area of diverse CLDHs was determined using a NOVA4200E microporous analyzer (Quantachrome, USA), and their pore size distributions were calculated using the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) theory.

According to the Chinese national standard GB/T 17671-1999, the compressive strength values of various specimens at 3-, 7-, 14-, and 28-d curing ages were measured. Isothermal calorimeters were used to monitor the heat evolution of hydration at a temperature of 20 ± 2 °C for 72 h, following the ASTMC 1702 standard. The specimen preparation was as follows: 6.0 g cement pastes were weighed for each group, and the stirring time was kept consistent for all groups. After approximately 10 s stirring, the mixture was immediately put into a closed container for thermal examination of hydration. Thermogravimetric analysis/differential thermogravimetric (TGA/DTG) and XRD techniques were employed to investigate the hydration process and chemical composition of the hydrated product phases. The sample preparation involved the steps blow: The specimens of 28-d curing cement pastes were immersed in anhydrous ethanol for 3 d so as to halt hydration process. After that, specimens were dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C until their mass remained constant. Subsequently, the dried paste specimens were uniformly crushed into test sample powder with an average particle diameter of less than 75 μm. During XRD measurements, the scan rate was set at 0.02° per step, along with a voltage of 40 kV and a current of 40 mA. Regarding TGA/DTG curves, they were recorded using an STA-409PC Thermogravimetric Analyzer (USA) in a flowing N2 atmosphere, and approximately 50 mg powder samples were adopted. Microstructures and morphology of the hydration products in 28-d curing cement pastes specimens were examined using MIRA LMS scanning electron microscopy (SEM) from TESCAN, Czech Republic. All specimens underwent gold spraying treatment before measurement to improve the quality of the SEM images. Additionally, the pore structure parameters of the cement mortar specimens were determined using the mercury intrusion porosimetry (MIP) with an AutoPore IV 9500 instrument (Micromeritics, USA). The samples were obtained by following: Cement mortar specimens were soaked in ethanol for one week, accompanied by continuous solvent replacement. And then the specimens were dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C until their mass was constant, and these dried specimens acted as samples.

To determine the chloride binding capacity of the cement composites, the diameter of cement paste powder was less than 75 μm, taken from the same specimens as the XRD tests described above. Specifically, 4 g cement powder was added to plastic centrifuge tube with 40 mL 3 M NaCl solution and maintained at an ambient temperature of 20 ± 2 °C. To avoid the precipitation of chlorine salts caused by water evaporation, all plastic centrifuge tubes must be kept sealed. Meanwhile, all centrifuge tubes were manually shaken for a constant time (3 min) every 24 h to ensure complete contact with chloride and cement powder. After 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 12, 14, and 28 d in 3 M NaCl solution, centrifugation was completed at 5000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was used to evaluate the residual free chloride ions and the solid phase was utilized for other analyses. Free chloride ions content in the supernatant was determined using an automatic potentiometric titrator with 0.1 M AgNO3. To ensure the reliability of the test results, each titration was calibrated with a standard NaCl solution of 3 M before each measurement, and eight measurements were conducted for each specimen. The corresponding binding chloride content (C b) was calculated using Equation (2):

where, C b , C 0, C 1, V 0, and m represented the bound chloride content (mg/g pastes), the initial concentration of chloride in NaCl solutions before immersion (M), the concentrations of initial NaCl solutions (M), the volume of NaCl solution used for immersion (mL) and the quality of cement paste powder (g), respectively.

To deeply explore the potential effect of dispersion-treated CLDH on the chloride-binding properties of cement pastes, the physical phases, structural compositions, and microstructures of various specimens after being immersed in 3 M NaCl solution were analyzed using XRD, FTIR, TGA/DTG, and SEM. The precipitates and supernatants were separated at 5000 rpm, and then centrifuged precipitates were dried under vacuum at 50 °C up to a constant mass. The obtained dry powder was used for related tests. Furthermore, to evaluate the percentage of the physical phase in cement pastes after chloride binding, the paste and ZnO were mixed thoroughly and homogeneously in a ratio of 4:1. Then quantitatively examined using the Rietveld method on XRD patterns. Simultaneously, the TGA curve mass loss was utilized to quantify chloride binding in each specimen, aiding in the identification of chloride binding in the cement pastes. It was hypothesized that the mass loss of Friedel’s salt (Fs) was related to the primary layer of water molecules within its structure, and calculate the amount of Fs in the cement pastes using Equation (3). Then, the binding amount of chloride by Fs was computed using Equation (4):

where, M Fs = 561 g/mol (the molar mass of Fs with the chemical composition of Ca4Al2Cl2(OH)12.4H2O or C3A.CaCl2 .10H2O); m H represented the mass loss of 6 molecules water in the Fs main layer structure (wt.%); M H = 18 g/mol (molar mass of H2O) and M cl = 35.5 g/mol (molar mass of Cl). Specifically, it should be particularly noted that the Cl/Al of 1 in Fs had been simplified.

The morphological and topographic characteristics of GQDs were assessed through TEM and AFM images. As displayed in TEM (Fig. 2 a) and high-resolution TEM image (HRTEM) images (Fig. 2a), GQDs were spherical and monodispersed with an average size of approximately 3.15 nm and a distribution ranging from 1.05 to 6.65 nm. Moreover, GQDs possessed a well-defined crystal structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite. The AFM image (Fig. 2c) further evidenced the excellent dispersion of GQDs. Additionally, the measured topographical highness of GQDs (Fig. 2d) varied from 0.5 nm to 1.3 nm, indicating the presence of single/few-layer GQDs.

To comprehensively analyze the structural information of GQDs, XRD, Raman and FTIR spectra were utilized to examine the prepared GQDs. The XRD pattern (Fig. 3 a) exhibited a significant broad peak of about 27.2°, consistent with the graphite (002) plane. In Fig. 3b, the Raman spectra at 1370 and 1574 cm−1 revealed two distinguishing peaks indicating the D-band (disordered sp3 defects) and G-band (sp2 carbon), respectively. With their ratio (I D /I G) was 0.87, further affirming the favorable crystallinity of GQDs. As in FTIR spectra (Fig. 3c), absorption bands by stretching vibration of –OH, C−OH and C−O−C groups were observed at 3444, 1388 and 1070 cm−1, respectively (Qiang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2014; Yan et al., 2020). These abundant oxygen-containing groups conferred superior solubility of GQDs in water. Besides, the band at 1592 cm−1 testified the presence of CC (Li et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2014), further confirming that GQDs had a sp2 carbon structure.

In summary, the synthesized GQDs were characterized by a typical amphiphilic structure, consisting of a hydrophilic carbon core with the single/few-layer graphene structure, containing hydrophilic oxygenous groups on the surface of core. Thus, this amphiphilic structure conduced to confer excellent exfoliation abilities on GQDs (Yan et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2019).

TEM analyses (Fig. 4 ) provided direct insights into the morphologies of different CLDHs. In Fig. 4a, CLDH with a hexagonal lamellar structure densely agglomerated and stacked in multiple layers. The diameter size of agglomerated CLDH was more than 1000 nm, larger than that of individual CLDH (200 nm). After pure ultrasonication, U-CLDH displayed a moderate reduction in agglomeration, but suffered from cross-linking, leading to the formation of elongated chains. Notably, its thickness appeared visibly thinner than CLDH (Fig. 4b). This observation indicated that ultrasonic treatment facilitated the dispersity of CLDH, resulting in reduced agglomeration and thickness. In sharp contrast to CLDH and U-CLDH, the agglomeration and thickness of G-CLDH were significantly reduced, shortening the cross-linking chains (Fig. 4c). Thus, G-CLDH was expected to possess markedly increased specific surface area. Additionally, the edge shape of hexagonal G-CLDH transformed from a regular shape to an irregular shape, indicating that the incorporation of GQDs modified the CLDH structure under ultrasonication. It is noteworthy that GQDs failed to be observed on the surface of G-CLDH, attributed to the difficulty in finding small GQDs with an average particle diameter of 3.15 nm at the given magnification (200 nm). All the findings above indicated that G-CLDH had excellent dispersive properties, testifying that the GQDs-assisted ultrasonic method was more efficient in exfoliating CLDH than pure ultrasonic treatment. The high dispersity of G-CLDH lays a solid foundation for enhancing the properties of cement composites.

The crystal structures of commercial Mg/Al-CO3 LDH and various CLDHs were examined using XRD (Fig. 5 a). In detail, Mg/Al-CO3 LDH revealed five distinct diffraction peaks at 11.7°, 23.5°, 34.6°, 60.8° and 62.2°, corresponding to planes of (003), (006), (012), (110) and (113), respectively (Fig. 5a). Upon calcination, the characteristic peaks associated with LDH disappeared (Fig. 5a), suggesting that the loss of anions and water molecules between hydrotalcite layers resulted in structure collapse and the formation of mixed oxides (Guo et al., 2020). While as for U-CLDH and G-CLDH, they experienced reconstitution during the ultrasonic treatment in the aqueous solution, leading to the reappearance of LDH characteristic peaks. This finding indicated that U-CLDH and G-CLDH with the favorable structural memory effect regained layer structures after ultrasonication. While U-CLDH and G-CLDH shared lower (003) peak than Mg/Al-CO3 LDH, indicating their incomplete crystal structure (Cai et al., 2022). Notably, compared with U-CLDH, the intensities of (003) and (006) peaks in G-CLDH decreased, suggesting that the GQDs-assisted ultrasound weakened the crystallinity of G-CLDH, occasioning its structure change. This finding well coincided with the result of TEM. Furthermore, due to the low content of GQDs, no diffraction peaks of GQDs were observed in XRD.

FTIR spectra (Fig. 5b) were utilized to examine the chemical structural compositions of LDH and CLDHs. Specifically, LDH, U-CLDH and G-CLDH shared same absorption peaks, indicating that U-CLDH and G-CLDH possessed similar structural compositions with LDH, owing to their reconstitution in the aqueous solution during ultrasonication. In stark contrast to those of LDH, the absorption peaks at 3400 cm−1 (corresponding to O-H of the interlayer water) and 1370 cm−1 (resulting from carbonate ions) of CLDHs became obviously weaker. This was attributed to the fact that LDH underwent dehydration and removal of the interlayer anion (CO3 2−) to form CLDH by calcination. Additionally, the absorption peaks in the range of 600–1000 cm−1 (attributed to M−O (M = Mg, Al)) found in both LDH and CLDHs (Ahmed and Gasser, 2012; Qu et al., 2018). It should be emphasized that due to their low doping concentration, the absorption peaks of GQDs were not detected in FTIR spectra.

To clarify the specific surface area (S BET) values and pore structures of LDH and CLDHs, the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and accompanying BJH pore size distributions were examined. As presented in Fig. 6 a, all the adsorption-desorption isotherms exhibited type-IV adsorption characteristics with H3-type hysteresis loops, indicating substantial mesopores in the layered LDH and CLDHs. Compared with CLDH and U-CLDH, the pore size distribution peak of G-CLDH was shifted to the right significantly (Fig. 6b), signifying that ultrasonic processing with the assistance of GQDs contributed to preparing CLDH with a greater layer spacing. Correspondingly, G-CLDH possessed the largest S BET value of 56.013 m2 g−1, boosting by 328.07 % and 92.04 % than LDH (13.085 m2 g−1) and U-CLDH (29.167 m2 g−1) respectively. This strongly substantiated that the GQDs-assisted ultrasonication provided more effective dispersity for CLDH, probably better for improving the properties of cement composites. Moreover, as for the pore volume (D V), G-CLDH exhibited a decreasing trend, reducing by 120.59 % and 84.49 %, compared with LDH and U-CLDH respectively. Correspondingly, the total pore size (V Total) of G-CLDH exhibited a significant increase of 94.23 % and 61.17 % than that of LDH and U-CLDH respectively (in Table 4 ). These findings unequivocally demonstrated that pure ultrasonic treatment could enhance the S BET and D V of U-CLDH, but its beneficial effect was limited; While after introducing GQDs, the S BET and D V of G-CLDH were further improved, offering a better dispersity for G-CLDH. This aligned with the result from TEM analyses.

To sum up, the analyses of TEM, XRD, FTIR, and nitrogen adsorption revealed that the size, thickness, and agglomeration of G-CLDH were remarkably lower than those of U-CLDH and Mg/Al-CO3 LDH. Thus, G-CLDH possessed the largest S BET value. This verified that the GQDs-assisted ultrasonication could effectively exfoliate the CLDH, and greatly promote its dispersion. The as-obtained highly dispersed G-CLDH was anticipated to significantly enhance the mechanical and chloride binding properties of cementitious composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Compressive strength and chloride binding capacity of cementitious composites
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the influence of three kinds of CLDHs on the mechanical properties of cementitious composites, it was assessed the compressive strength of mortar specimens containing 0.8 wt.% CLDH/U-CLDH/G-CLDH at 3-, 7-, 14-, and 28-d curing. As depicted in Fig. 7 , all the CLDHs demonstrated a positive effect on the compressive strength of mortar specimens, especially on the later compressive strength. Specifically, after 28-d curing, GC group uncovered the biggest compressive strength of 56.5 MPa, followed by UC (50.8 MPa) and C specimens (48.5 MPa). These enhanced by 22.3 %, 10.0 % and 5.0 % compared to that of B group (46.2 MPa), indicative of the positive correlation between compressive strength of mortars and the dispersion degree of CLDH. In other words, the higher the dispersion of CLDH, the better the compressive strength enhancement of mortar specimens, especially at later stage. It must be emphasized that the pure incorporation of GQDs presented a negative effect on the compressive strength regardless of the curing ages. For instance, the compressive strength of G group at 28-d curing was only 43.7 MPa, representing a decrease of 5.4 % than that of B group. This implied that the evident increase of compressive strength for GC group was only ascribed to the addition of G-CLDH.

To investigate the effect of various CLDHs on chloride binding, the chloride binding (C b) values of different cement pastes were tested using typical equilibrium test. Fig. 8 a presented the C b values obtained through Equation (1). The C b values of all samples increased over immersion times. Meanwhile, the C b values of cement pastes containing CLDHs with different dispersion treatments were also varied. Among them, GC group owned the highest C b value of 40.67 mg/g, followed by UC group (34.58 mg/g) and C group (30.57 mg/g), increasing by 58.6 %, 34.8 % and 19.2 %, respectively compared with B group (25.65 mg/g). This indicated that there was a positive correlation between the chloride binding capacity of cement pastes with the dispersity degree of CLDHs. It should be noted that unlike the compressive strength test, adding GQDs promoted the chloride binding of cement pastes. After 28-d immersion, the C b value of G group was 28.51 mg/g, increasing by 11.2 % more than B group. This indicated that GQDs were beneficial to improve the chloride binding, but G-CLDH predominated the enhancement of chloride binding for GC group. In addition, as displayed in Fig. 8b, all groups had higher increase rate of C b values in the first 5 d in 3 M NaCl solution. Especially, GC group exhibited the highest increase rate of 74.6 % after 3-d immersion. These results indicated that cement composites containing G-CLDHs were capable of more rapidly immobilizing free chloride ions at an early stage.

The observations above suggested that highly dispersed G-CLDH could simultaneously enhance the mechanical and chloride binding properties of cementitious composites, fully verifying the feasibility and efficiency of the proposed GQDs-assisted ultrasonic dispersion method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydration heat evolution, phase composition and microstructure of various composite cement pastes
ce:section.ce:para: To acquire a better understanding of the effect of various CLDHs on compressive strength and chloride binding capacity, a comprehensive battery of tests and analyses were conducted on diverse pastes specimens, including hydration heat, physical phase composition and microstructure.

As is well known, the cement hydration is an exothermic process. Thus, observing changes in the hydration heat provides a route to effectively characterize the hydration evolution of cement pastes. The cumulative heat release and exothermic rate of different cement pastes during the initial 72 h of hydration were depicted In Fig. 9 . Compared to the B group (113.12 J/g), all the CLDH-containing groups exhibited higher cumulative heat release; And GC group achieved the highest value of 152.69 J/g, followed by UC (146.05 J/g) and C (128.72 J/g) (Fig. 9a), increasing by 34.98 %, 29.11 % and 13.79 % respectively. This finding demonstrated that different CLDHs could effectively promote the early hydration of cement. Among them, G-CLDH exhibited the most obvious promotion, indicating that G-CLDH with better dispersity could more markedly promote cement hydration. As illustrated in Fig. 9b, the maximum exothermic peak intensities of CLDH-containing groups increased than B group. Among them, GC group showed the largest maximum exothermic peak (0.00222 W/g), followed by UC group (0.00189 W/g) and C group (0.00168 W/g), increasing by 52.05 %, 29.45 % and 15.07 % than B group (0.00146 W/g), respectively. Furthermore, compared with B group, the exothermic peaks of C, UC and GC groups shifted to left from 18.11 to 16.23, 16.03 and 15.66 h respectively. This result further indicated the promotion of cement hydration by CLDHs was related to their dispersity degree. Highly dispersed G-CLDH could more significantly accelerate the reaction rate. In addition, regarding G group, GQDs increased the cumulative heat release (Fig. 9a), and slightly increased the intensity of the maximum exothermic peak, but marginally delayed the appearance of maximum exothermic peak, (Fig. 9b) compared to B group. This suggested that GQDs had a limited impact on the maximum exothermic peak of GC group. And G-CLDH was primarily responsible for the improvement of cumulative heat release and exothermic rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase composition of specimens before chloride binding
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR, XRD and TGA/DTG techniques were employed to analyze the phase formation of 28-d curing cement pastes. As displayed in FTIR spectra (Fig. 10 a), all the groups shared the same characteristic peaks at 1424, 1114, 957 and 873 cm−1, associated with CO3 2− of CaCO3 (carbonation reaction between atmospheric CO2 and Ca(OH)2), S–O of AFt/AFm and Si–O of C–S–H gels respectively (Qu et al., 2021). This indicated that the introduction of CLDHs or GQDs failed to alter the types of hydration products. XRD patterns further supported the finding from FTIR spectra (Fig. 10b). Similar peaks corresponding to Ca(OH)2 (peaks at 18.1°, 28.7°, 34°, and 47.1°), the unhydrated products dicalcium silicate (C2S, peaks at 32° and 41.2°), and tricalcium silicate (C3S, peak at 32.6°) were observed in all the samples. Generally, the major diffraction peak Ca(OH)2 at 18.1° is directly related to the amount of hydration production, and the intensity of this peak can be used to determine the hydration degree (Hu et al., 2019). Apparently, the peak at 18.1° illustrated a notable change among the different groups. Compared with B group, CLDH-containing groups exhibited more sharper peak of Ca(OH)2. This was attributed to the CLDH-induced acceleration of cement hydration, leading to more formation of C-S-H gels and Ca(OH)2 (Yang, L. et al., 2021b). Moreover, the peak intensity of Ca(OH)2 gradually increased from C, UC to GC, implying that CLDH with higher dispersity had a better promotion of Ca(OH)2 production. This result was coincident with the analyses of hydration heat evolution. In addition, the peak intensity of Ca(OH)2 was also increased in G group, probably related to the fact that GQDs served as nucleation sites to promote the cement hydration process to some extent (Long et al., 2022a; Qu et al., 2022).

Since XRD exhibited limited sensitivity toward minor and amorphous phases (Wang et al., 2023), TGA/DTG tests were conducted to further acquire the exhaustive information of phases (Fig. 11 ). As shown in Fig. 11a, all specimens exhibited a similar decreasing trend in the TGA curves. CLDHs-containing groups uncovered more mass loss compared to the blank group, implying that the cement pastes containing CLDHs contained more hydration products. Among them, the GC group exhibited the most significant mass loss, indicating that the highest amount of hydration products in the GC group. In the DTG curves (Fig. 11b), different phases could be distinguished based on the mass loss peaks in specific temperature intervals. Although the DTG curves of all specimens exhibited similar mass loss peaks, the intensity of these peaks varied in specific temperature intervals, suggesting that the addition of CLDHs did not modify the types of hydration products, but influenced the content of hydration products. This finding corresponded to the results of FTIR and XRD analyses. In detail, the disintegration of C-S-H/AFt, Ms, AFm, Ca(OH)2 and CaCO3 produced mass loss peaks in the ranges of 105–114 °C, 150–180 °C, 350–400 °C, 400–550 °C and 550–800 °C, respectively (Justnes et al., 2020; Lanas and Alvarez, 2004). All CLDHs-containing groups exhibited significantly larger mass loss peaks of C-S-H/AFt than B group, and these peaks in GC group were the most significant, followed by UC and C group. It could be inferred that the amount of C-S-H/AFt was related to the dispersity degree of CLDH. The higher the dispersity degree of CLDH, the more C-S-H gels formed. The generation of more C-S-H gels led to a denser microstructure in cement matrix, thus contributing to promoting the compressive strength. Additionally, compared with B group, the mass loss peak of C-S-H/AFt was slightly larger in G group indicated that GQDs could also promoted cement hydration and generate a larger number of C-S-H gels to some extent, and G-CLDH predominated the increment of C-S-H gels for GC group.

Overall, the addition of CLDHs did not affect the type of cement hydration products, but promoted the hydration process and the content of hydration products. In particular, highly dispersed G-CLDH significantly accelerated the cement hydration, increased the cumulative heat release and generated more C-S-H gels, thus conducing to improving the compressive strength. These results further indicated that the GQDs-assisted ultrasonic method to disperse CLDH was effective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase composition of specimens after chloride binding
ce:section.ce:para: To reveal the mechanism of chloride binding for CLDHs-modified cement pastes, FTIR spectra, XRD patterns and TGA/DTG curves were conducted to the phase compositions of cement pastes immersed in 3 M NaCl solution for 28 d. The FTIR spectra (Fig. 12 a) showed that all groups exhibited the same characteristic peaks, indicating that they contained the same hydration products. All groups showed a new peak at 798 cm−1 owing to Al-OH in Friedel’s (Fs) (Geng et al., 2021), apart from the peaks at 1424, 957 and 873 cm−1 caused by Si-O (C-S-H gels) and CO3 2− (CaCO3). According to the previous literature (Guo et al., 2022), the monosulfate (AFm) phase reacted with free chloride ions to primarily form Fs when the free chloride ions content was exceeded 1 mol/L. The free chloride content used in this paper was 3 mol/L. Hence, all cement pastes mainly generated Fs after immersion in NaCl solution to achieve chloride chemical binding. XRD patterns further verified the existence of Fs (Fig. 12b). Compared with the pre-immersion period (Fig. 10a), all specimens showed new peaks of diffraction at 11°, 22°, and 23°, suggesting that all the cement pastes generated Fs after immersion. This further verified that chloride chemical binding was accomplished.

The Rietveld method was used to quantitatively analyze XRD spectra for further revealing phase compositions in the different groups after 28-d immersion. As shown in Fig. 12c, with the dispersity degree of CLDH, the percentage of unhydrated products (C3S and C2S) decreased. Conversely, the content of C-S-H gels showed an increasing trend. This further indicated that highly dispersed G-CLDH was favorable to improve cement hydration, thereby promoting the generation of C-S-H gels. Specifically, the C-S-H gels content in GC, UC and C groups were 58.71 %, 57.31 % and 55.93 %, respectively, improving by 12.95 %, 10.25 % and 7.60 % compared with B group (51.98 %). In addition, the C-S-H gels content in G group (54.57 %) was also higher than that of B group, indicating that GQDs also promoted cement hydration and generate C-S-H gels. As for Fs, its content was 9.29 %, 9.17 %, 9.06 %, 8.43 % and 8.20 % for GC, UC, C, G, and B groups, respectively. This revealed that CLDHs especially G-CLDH could boost the generation of Fs, but the increase of Fs was not significant. Overall, G-CLDH with higher dispersity could promote the hydration of cement to generate more C-S-H gels, thus enhancing the physically adsorbed chloride ions. Meanwhile, G-CLDH could slightly enhance the chloride chemical binding due to an indistinctive increase of Fs.

TGA/DTG was used to analyze detailedly the exact phases in different cement pastes after 28-d immersion. In TGA curves (Fig. 13 a), all groups showed a similar decreasing trend, and GC group possessed the highest mass loss. This implied that the addition of CLDHs could not change the type of hydration products, but affected the amount of hydration products. And GC group contained the most hydration products. Compared with the DTG curves of pre-immersion groups (Fig. 11b), new mass loss peaks at the ranges of 140–230 °C, 260–370 °C and 610–740 °C were found in the DTG curves after 28-d immersion, belonging to Fs/monosulfate (Ms), Fs, and CaCO3 respectively (Fig. 13b). The mass loss peaks of C-S-H/AFt at 105–114 °C in CLDHs-containing groups were strengthened than that of B group, which indicated that adding CLDHs could increase the content of C-S-H gels and consequently improving the physical adsorption of chloride ions. Among them, the GC group held the most significant mass loss peaks of C-S-H/AFt, suggesting that G-CLDH had the largest chloride physical adsorption induced by C-S-H gels. In addition, the mass loss peaks of Fs/Ms and Fs in CLDHs-modified groups were marginally stronger than B group (He et al., 2023b; Krishnan and Bishnoi, 2018; Shi et al., 2017), demonstrating that the addition of CLDHs promoted the formation of Fs, thereby slightly increasing chemically bound chloride ions. These findings were agreed with the quantitative analysis of XRD. It is worth nothing that the mass loss peak of C-S-H/AFt was slightly larger than B group, indicating that GQDs could also promote the cement hydration and produce C-S-H gels to some extent, but the improvement effect was not significant due to the small dosage, and G-CLDH predominated the increment of C-S-H gels for GC group.

As is well known, the chloride binding in cement composites is mainly categorized into two types: physical adsorption and chemical binding. Higher chloride concentrations cause Fs to dominated the latter, while C-S-H gels control the former. Apart from Fs and C-S-H gels, other hydration products show an insignificant influence on the chloride binding capability of cement matrix (Long et al., 2022b). While in this study, owing to the ion exchange capacity of CLDH, the CLDH-induced adsorbed chloride ions were assigned to the physical adsorption. Thus, chloride physical adsorption included two parts: CLDH-induced adsorption and C-S-H-caused adsorption, noted as
C b

To sum up, after 28-d immersion, the presence of Fs in the cement pastes achieved chloride chemical binding. Compared with B group, the amount of chloride binding was enhanced in both CLDHs-containing groups. The chloride binding in different cement pastes was mainly dominated by chloride physical adsorption. Among them, GC group had the highest amount of physical adsorption and chemical binding of chloride ions, indicating that G-CLDH dispersed by GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment primarily enhanced the physical adsorption of chloride ions by cement pastes, and assisted in the enhancement in chemical binding chloride ions to some extent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructural analyses of specimens before chloride binding
ce:section.ce:para: SEM and MIP were used to analyze the effects of CLDHs on the microstructures of cement pastes at 28-d curing age. Fig. 14 demonstrated SEM images of varied cement pastes. For B group (Fig. 14a), hydration products were irregularly distributed, and consisted of a small amount of flaky Ca(OH)2 embedded in the matrix, as well as sporadic distribution of larger granular C-S-H gels. Similarly, flaky Ca(OH)2 attached to the surface of unhydrated products, and some granular C-S-H gels could be observed in G group (Fig. 14b). In addition, numerous pores existed in the matrix, may affecting the its mechanical properties. This indicated that adding a little dosage of GQDs (0.032 wt.%) could promote the hydration of cement and generated more C-S-H gels to some extent, but introduced some pores, thus negatively affecting the compressive strength of GQDs-modified mortar. Regarding C group (Fig. 14c), the agglomeration of CLDH occurred in the matrix, limiting the full utilization of its excellent properties. For UC group (Fig. 14d), a relatively larger number of small granular C-S-H gels attached to the matrix surface, along with a few CLDH partially covered by granular C-S-H gels. Regarding the GC group (Fig. 14e), the hydration products were uniformly dispersed, primarily consisting of a relatively large number of granular C-S-H gels. This was beneficial to forming a dense internal structure. Additionally, no G-CLDH was observed in the GC group, indicating that the dense C-S-H gels may entirely cover G-CLDH. These observations suggested that G-CLDH with higher dispersity was favorable to promote the generation of C-S-H gels, and effectively filled pores, resulting in a denser microstructure within cement matrix.

Pore characteristics significantly influence the compressive strength. Therefore, the effect of CLDHs on the pore structures were analyzed by MIP method. Fig. 15 presented the pore size distribution and pore volume of various mortars. And the total porosity and average pore size, calculated from the MIP results were listed in Table 5 . Compared with B group, the CLDH-containing groups showed a decrease in total porosity, average pore diameter and median pore diameter. Among them, GC group shown the minimum total porosity of 9.48 %, followed by UC group (11.51 %) and C (12.06 %), decreased by 54.66 %, 44.95 % and 42.32 % compared to B group (20.91 %), respectively. However, the total porosity of G group was up to 21.67 %, higher than B group. This finding inferred that the highly dispersed G-CLDH not GQDs endowed GC group with a denser microstructure, thus favorable to improve the compressive strength of mortar. And this enhancement could offset the negative effects induced by GQDs. Correspondingly, GC group (41.8 nm) had the smallest average pore diameter, followed by UC group (45.6 nm) and C group (49.5 nm), which decreased by 22.01 %, 14.93 % and 7.65 % respectively than B group (53.6 nm). In addition, the trend of median pore diameter in all the groups was similar to that of average pore diameter, decreasing successively from B group (194.2 nm), C group (156.2 nm), UC group (152.4 nm) to GC group (148.9 nm). This result disclosed that dispersity degree of CLDHs mainly determined the pore structures of mortars, and G-CLDH with the highest dispersity could more effectively exert the nano-nucleation and filling effects. As exhibited in Fig. 15b, the most frequent pore diameter value (113.73 nm) was the smallest in GC group, significantly decreasing by 66.37 %, followed by UC group (66.36 %) and C group (57.38 %), compared to B group (338.23 nm). However, the most frequent pore diameter value of G group (1259.32 nm) significantly increased by 272.24 % than that of B group. In general, the smaller values of the most frequent pore diameters, the denser pore structure of mortar. It further proved that highly dispersed G-CLDH was more favorable for refining the pore structure, thereby promoting the development of mechanical strength.

In brief, the CLDHs-containing groups exhibited denser microstructures, lower total porosities, average pore diameters and median pore diameters. Among them, GC group possessed the minimum total porosity, average pore diameter and median pore diameter, indicating that the higher dispersed G-CLDH was beneficial to promoting cement hydration and filling the pores, thereby forming a denser microstructure within cement matrix. This denser microstructure further leaded to a higher compressive strength on the macro level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructural analyses of specimens after chloride binding
ce:section.ce:para: SEM images were used to observe the morphological characteristics of hydration products inside cement pastes after 28-d immersion (Fig. 16 ). Granular C-S-H gels were observed in all specimens. The morphology of these granular-like C-S-H gels corresponds with the description of C-S-H gels morphology found in the literature (Sekkal et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2022b). Furthermore, the EDS analysis conducted on the GC samples reinforces our confidence that these granular-like substances were indeed C-S-H gels (Fig. 16g–i). In B group (Fig. 16a), some nanoparticulate C-S-H gels attached to the surface of the unhydrated cement clinker. The number of C-S-H gels in G group (Fig. 16b) was more than B group, indicating that the introduction of GQDs promoted the generation of C-S-H gels, thus enhancing the physical adsorption of chloride by the cement paste. For the specimens containing CLDHs, CLDHs could be observed in addition to the granular C-S-H gels. The higher dispersity the CLDHs, the more the granular C-S-H gels in the cement paste, agreeing with the results of TGA/DTG curves and the XRD quantitative analyses. Specifically, in C group (Fig. 16c), a large number of aggregated CLDH could be observe. At the same time, a few granular C-S-H gels could be observed on the surface of CLDH. In contrast, the distribution of U-CLDH in the cement matrix were dispersed without significant aggregation (Fig. 16d). Additionally, some granular C-S-H gels distributed on the surface of U-CLDH and inside the cement matrix, indicating that the pure ultrasonication could promote the dispersity of CLDH in the cement matrix. Regarding GC group (Fig. 16e and f), G-CLDH was almost completely disappeared, and granular C-S-H gels uniformly dispersed throughout the cement matrix. This proved that G-CLDH could be dispersed homogeneously throughout the cement matrix, promoting the cement pastes hydration, and generating abundant C-S-H gels. This contributed to the physical adsorption of chloride by the cement pastes. As the degree of CLDH dispersion was increased, the binding capacity of cement pastes for chloride also increased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance improvement mechanism of CLDHs-modified cement composites
ce:section.ce:para: Through comprehensively analyzing the characteristics of diverse CLDHs as well as the hydration heat evolutions, phase compositions and microstructures of various cement specimens, the CLDHs-induced improvement mechanisms referring to mechanics and chloride binding were rationally disclosed.

The mechanical enhancement mechanism of mortar induced by highly dispersed G-CLDH was revealed (Fig. 17 ). That was G-CLDH with high dispersion not only promoted the hydration of cement and generated a larger number of C-S-H gels, but only uniformly dispersed in the cement matrix to fill pores. The two synergistic effects resulted in the highest compressive strength of specimens containing G-CLDH. In detail, based on the results of TEM and nitrogen adsorption-desorption analyses, G-CLDH obtained by GQDs-assisted ultrasonication had the largest S BET and the highest dispersity, followed by U-CLDH and CLDH. It is well known that nanomaterials can provide more nucleation sites for nucleation, precipitation, and growth of hydration products (i.e., the nucleation effect), thus significantly contributing to cement hydration. Furthermore, the uniform distribution of nanomaterials in cement matrix could further fill the pores (Sun et al., 2020, 2022). Therefore, the incorporation of CLDH into cement matrix could promote cement hydration, and refine microstructures of the cement composites. However, owing to its serious agglomeration, CLDH failed to fully utilize the positive effects of nucleation and filling effects, resulting in stress concentration and only slightly increased the compressive strength (Fig. 17b). While as for U-CLDH (Fig. 17c), with the dispersity degree increasing, the cement hydration was further improved, prompting a further increase in compressive strength as well. With respect to G-CLDH (Fig. 17d), it possessed the largest S BET and highest degree of dispersion, which could better exert the nucleation effect and promote the hydration of cement and generate a larger number of C-S-H gels, confirmed by XRD and DTG. A larger number of C-S-H gels could refine the microstructure of cement matrix. Additionally, the highly dispersed G-CLDH could uniformly distribute in the cement matrix to fill the pores. This was proved by SEM and MIP. This indicated that increasing dispersion degree of CLDH contributed to improving the compressive strength of cementitious composites. Thus, G-CLDH dispersed by GQDs-assisted ultrasonic processing could more significantly increase the compressive strength of cementitious composites, leading to the fact that GC group had the highest compressive strength.

The G-CLDH-caused chloride binding improvement mechanism of cement pastes was rationally proposed (Fig. 18 ). Namely, highly dispersed G-CLDH not only promoted the forming of C-S-H gels, but also fully exerted ion exchange performance. Under the synergistic effect of chloride physical adsorption by C-S-H gels and ion exchange through highly dispersed G-CLDH, the chloride binding capacity of cement pastes was dramatically improved. DTG, SEM and quantitative calculations revealed that the values of chemically binding chloride did not change remarkably in all groups. On the contrary, the changes in the amount of physical adsorption chloride values were noticeable. Therefore, G-CLDH mainly affected physically adsorbed chloride ions. Specifically, the amount of chloride binding in B group was mainly through C-S-H gels by cement hydration (Fig. 18a). Since CLDH and U-CLDH showed relatively serious aggregation in cement matrix, leading to their limited contact area with chloride ions. Consequently, fewer chloride ions were bound by ion exchange. Additionally, the aggregation of CLDH/U-CLDH failed to efficiently increase the amounts of C-S-H gels generated by cement hydration, thereby reducing the amount of physically adsorbed chloride ions through C-S-H gels (Fig. 18b). Furthermore, the contact area of highly dispersed G-CLDH with chloride ions was significantly increased in GC group, causing an enhancement of chloride binding by ion exchange. Meanwhile, the highly dispersed G-CLDH also promoted cement hydration and generated a larger number of C-S-H gels, enhancing chloride binding by physical adsorption (Fig. 18c). Based on the literature (Long et al., 2023b), GQDs could also act as nucleation sites for cement hydration, generating more C-S-H gels. Hence, GC group had the highest chloride binding content.

This work developed a novel GQDs-assisted ultrasonic method to efficiently prepare high-dispersity G-CLDH, and demonstrated that the incorporation of 0.8 wt.% G-CLDH could markedly improve the compressive strength and chloride binding properties of cementitious composites for the first time. Most importantly, its related enhancement mechanisms involving mechanics and chloride binding were uncovered according to comprehensive analyses of CLDHs characterization as well as the evolutions of hydration heat, the compositions of phase, as well as microstructures of various cement pastes. Major conclusions were summarized as follows:
(1)
The generated GQDs had a hydrophilic carbon core with single/few-layer graphene structure with hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups on the surface of core, forming a characteristic amphiphilic structure. This amphiphilic structure endowed GQDs with excellent exfoliation ability to effectively exfoliate and disperse CLDH.
(2)
Highly dispersed G-CLDH possessed a larger S BET of 56.013 m2 g−1, significantly increasing by 92.04 % than U-CLDH. This suggested that GQDs-assisted ultrasonic treatment was an effective technique to simply obtain high-dispersion CLDH.
(3)
Regardless of curing ages, GC group containing 0.8 wt.% G-CLDH had the highest compressive strength and the largest C b value. Specifically, at 28-d curing age, GC group showed the highest compressive strength of 56.5 MPa, followed by UC group (50.8 MPa), improving by 22.3 % and 10.0 % respectively compared with B group (46.2 MPa). Moreover, as for the chloride binding, GC group also held the highest C b value of 40.67 mg/g, followed by UC group (34.58 mg/g), enhancing by 58.6 % and 34.8 %, respectively.
(4)
Based on comprehensively analyzing characterization of different CLDHs as well as the evolutions of hydration heat, the compositions of phase and microstructures of various cement pastes, the improvement mechanisms induced by G-CLDH were reasonably proposed. With reference to the mechanics, it was attributed to a significant decrease in porosity and a remarkable increase in C-S-H gels; Regarding chloride binding, a larger number of C-S-H gels and better dispersed G-CLDH led to more physically adsorbed chloride and more ion-exchanged-caused bound chloride respectively.

This study provided an innovative approach for efficiently dispersing CLDH, and verified the contribution of highly dispersed G-CLDH to the mechanical and chloride binding properties of cementitious composites. Both of these discoveries would contribute to the advancement of high-performance development in cement composites. Additionally, only specific ultrasonic parameter (720 W, 30 min) was used to disperse CLDH in this study. Therefore, it is reasonable to believe that the dispersity of CLDH is expected to be further improved by varying the parameters of ultrasonic power, ultrasonic time, and the dosage of GQDs, thus more efficiently enhancing the mechanical and chloride binding properties of cementitious composites.

Wu-Jian Long: Funding acquisition. Jian-Wei Liu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Shu-Yi Zheng: Supervision, Validation. Chuang He: Data curation, Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing – review & editing.
